title: Usability
body:
## What is usability?

### Techniques that help people perform tasks in computer-based graphical environments.

Three very important concepts. We work for **people** who want to perform a **task in a simple and effective way**, and in this particular case, they must do so on a computer in a **graphical environment—the web**. Usability helps ensure this task is performed easily by analyzing human behavior and the steps needed to complete the task effectively. It might sound a bit like industrial work, but when we use the term "task," we’re referring to finding a TV show we want to watch, looking up information about my favorite soccer team, or buying a book on martial arts. **Is usability really necessary for building a website?**

> Yes. Whether your website is a portal or a personal page, don’t forget that people visit your site because they’re basically looking for something. Making it simple is your decision, but together we can surely make the internet a better place where people don’t feel lost, confused, or simply disappointed.

**Where can I find more information on this topic?**

> There are thousands of websites that discuss usability on the internet. On the [links of interest] page (http://www.google.com/custom?domains=www.xarop.com&q=usabilidad+web&sa=Buscar&sitesearch=&client=pub-8162252435022913&forid=1&ie=ISO-8859-1&oe=ISO-8859-1&cof=&hl=es), available in this manual, you’ll find a selection of sites that have helped me learn and study this topic.

**What are the 8 Golden Rules of Usability?**

> I hope you find them useful. Maybe tomorrow there will be 10 rules. **1.** **On the internet, the user is in charge.** This means that without users, your website dies, so you’d better take care of them and give them what they ask for; otherwise, you’ll be left alone. **2.** **On the internet, quality is based on speed and reliability.** On the internet, it matters more that your site is fast than pretty, reliable than modern, simple than complex, and straightforward. **3.** **Security.** If in the real world we sometimes struggle to trust the bank around the corner, imagine how people feel online when they visit your site. Make sure everything runs like clockwork so people can trust your site. **4.** **Trust is hard to earn and can be lost with a single broken link.** This means that given the competition online, you can’t afford to lose a single visitor because of a broken link. It’s better to start with something simple and gradually make it more complex than to go all out and see “what happens.” Version 1.0 is fine as long as what’s in place is solid and builds trust. Little by little, and with user feedback, you can make the site more complex. But play it safe before taking risks. **5. If you want to build a decent website, simplify, streamline, and optimize.** People aren’t going to learn your site no matter how much you insist, so at least make it simple—reuse as many elements as you can—so that users feel comfortable and don’t get lost every time they need to find something on your site. **6. Put the conclusions at the beginning.** Users will feel more comfortable if they see the goals right away. This way, they won’t have to search for what they need and will spend less time completing their task. If they complete their task faster, they’ll feel satisfied and might explore your site further or even recommend it to a friend. **7\. Don’t waste people’s time with things they don’t need.** Be careful with cross-promotions; if you do them, at least do so with care. Make sure the selection of products you cross-promote is consistent, and don’t try to “sell everything” on every page. As the user navigates further, try to leave them more open space. It could happen that just as they’re about to buy something, they see an offer that distracts them, and you lose that sale. **8\. Good content.** Writing well for the internet is an art form. But by following the basic rules of (1) putting the conclusions at the beginning and (2) writing about 25% of what you would put on paper, you can go a long way. Reading on a screen is hard work, so when it comes to web content, cut down and simplify as much as you can.
